questions.text: What is the primary difference between a void function and a value-returning function?

choices.label: Void functions cannot have parameters.
choices.text: Void functions cannot have parameters.

choices.label: Void functions do not return a value and are called as stand-alone statements.
choices.text: Void functions do not return a value and are called as stand-alone statements.

choices.label: Void functions must be defined before the main function.
choices.text: Void functions must be defined before the main function.

choices.label: Value-returning functions cannot use reference parameters.
choices.text: Value-returning functions cannot use reference parameters.

questions.explanation: A void function does not have a return type and is invoked as a complete statement, whereas value-returning functions return a value that is usually part of an expression.

questions.text: Which symbol is used to declare a reference parameter in a C++ function heading?
questions.explanation: The ampersand (&) symbol following the data type in a formal parameter list indicates that the parameter is a reference parameter.

questions.text: In C++, the scope of a local identifier is:

choices.label: The entire program file.
choices.text: The entire program file.

choices.label: From its declaration to the end of the block in which it is declared.
choices.text: From its declaration to the end of the block in which it is declared.

choices.label: Only within the 'main' function.
choices.text: Only within the 'main' function.

choices.label: Across all functions in the program regardless of where it is declared.
choices.text: Across all functions in the program regardless of where it is declared.

questions.explanation: Local identifiers are accessible only within the block (curly braces) where they are defined, starting from the point of declaration.

questions.text: What is the 'signature' of a function in the context of function overloading?

choices.label: The return type and the function name.
choices.text: The return type and the function name.

choices.label: The function name and the formal parameter list.
choices.text: The function name and the formal parameter list.

choices.label: The memory address of the function.
choices.text: The memory address of the function.

choices.label: The function name, return type, and parameter list.
choices.text: The function name, return type, and parameter list.

questions.explanation: The signature consists of the function name and the number and types of its formal parameters. It does NOT include the return type.

questions.text: What is the rule for assigning default values to parameters in a function prototype?

choices.label: Default parameters must be the leftmost parameters.
choices.text: Default parameters must be the leftmost parameters.

choices.label: All parameters must have default values if one does.
choices.text: All parameters must have default values if one does.

choices.label: Default parameters must be the rightmost parameters.
choices.text: Default parameters must be the rightmost parameters.

choices.label: Default parameters can only be used with value-returning functions.
choices.text: Default parameters can only be used with value-returning functions.

questions.explanation: C++ requires that all default parameters be the rightmost parameters in the formal parameter list.

questions.text: Which of the following is true about static variables declared within a block?

choices.label: Memory is deallocated when the block is exited.
choices.text: Memory is deallocated when the block is exited.

choices.label: Memory remains allocated as long as the program executes.
choices.text: Memory remains allocated as long as the program executes.

choices.label: They are globally accessible by any function.
choices.text: They are globally accessible by any function.

choices.label: They are initialized every time the block is entered.
choices.text: They are initialized every time the block is entered.

questions.explanation: Static variables retain their memory and value throughout the entire execution of the program, even after the function or block they reside in finishes execution.

questions.text: What is a 'stub' in the context of program debugging?

choices.label: A program used to test another function.
choices.text: A program used to test another function.

choices.label: A placeholder function that is not fully coded but allows the rest of the program to be tested.
choices.text: A placeholder function that is not fully coded but allows the rest of the program to be tested.

choices.label: The final compiled version of the code.
choices.text: The final compiled version of the code.

choices.label: A logic error that causes the program to crash.
choices.text: A logic error that causes the program to crash.

questions.explanation: A stub is a dummy function used during development to stand in for a function that has not yet been implemented.

questions.text: Which operator is used to access a global variable when a local variable with the same name exists?
questions.explanation: The scope resolution operator (::) allows the program to bypass local scope and access a global identifier.

questions.text: What happens when a 'value parameter' is passed to a function?

choices.label: The function receives the memory address of the actual parameter.
choices.text: The function receives the memory address of the actual parameter.

choices.label: The function receives a copy of the content of the actual parameter.
choices.text: The function receives a copy of the content of the actual parameter.

choices.label: Any changes to the parameter inside the function affect the original variable.
choices.text: Any changes to the parameter inside the function affect the original variable.

choices.label: It saves memory space and time compared to a reference parameter.
choices.text: It saves memory space and time compared to a reference parameter.

questions.explanation: Value parameters create a local copy of the data. Changes made to the local copy do not affect the actual parameter in the calling function.

questions.text: Why should stream variables (like ifstream) be passed by reference?

choices.label: Because stream variables cannot be copied.
choices.text: Because stream variables cannot be copied.

choices.label: To make the program run slower.
choices.text: To make the program run slower.

choices.label: Because they are always global variables.
choices.text: Because they are always global variables.

choices.label: They don't have to be; passing by value is preferred.
choices.text: They don't have to be; passing by value is preferred.

questions.explanation: Stream objects maintain state information that cannot be easily copied; therefore, C++ requires them to be passed by reference.
'x' is passed by value, so the original 'x' remains 5. 'y' is passed by reference, so 'b = b + 10' updates the original 'y' to 15.
Because 'count' is static, it is initialized once and retains its value between function calls. Each call increments the existing value.
The local 'x' (20) hides the global 'x'. Using 'x' refers to the local version, while '::x' uses the scope resolution operator to access the global version (10).

choices.label: Error: Too few arguments
choices.text: Error: Too few arguments

questions.explanation: The value 1 is assigned to 'a', 2 overrides the default value of 'b', and 'c' uses its default value of 10.5.
First call: num becomes 20 (10*2). Second call: num becomes 40 (20*2) because it is passed by reference.

questions.text: Which of these function calls is ILLEGAL given the prototype: \n`void sample(int x, double y = 1.1, char z = 'A');`?

choices.label: sample(5);
choices.text: sample(5);

choices.label: sample(5, 2.2);
choices.text: sample(5, 2.2);

choices.label: sample(5, , 'B');
choices.text: sample(5, , 'B');

choices.label: sample(1, 2, 'C');
choices.text: sample(1, 2, 'C');

questions.explanation: You cannot skip a middle default argument. If you want to provide a value for 'z', you must also provide a value for 'y'.

choices.label: Compiler Error
choices.text: Compiler Error

questions.explanation: Since 'x' is a value parameter, the function 'change' only modifies its local copy. 'val' remains 50.

choices.label: Compilation Error
choices.text: Compilation Error

questions.explanation: The compiler uses function overloading resolution. Since 5 and 10 are integers, it calls the 'int' version of the function.
'x' is an automatic variable local to the for-loop block. It is re-initialized to 0 in every iteration of the loop.
'a' is passed by reference (x), so 10 + 20 = 30 is stored in 'a'. 'b' is passed by value (y), so modifications to 'y' do not affect 'b'.

choices.label: Outputs 5
choices.text: Outputs 5

choices.label: Outputs the address of x
choices.text: Outputs the address of x

choices.label: Compilation Error
choices.text: Compilation Error

choices.label: Runtime Error
choices.text: Runtime Error

questions.explanation: You cannot declare a local variable with the same name as a formal parameter within the same function scope.
Because they are reference parameters, the values of 'u' and 'v' are successfully swapped in main.
The function 'update' modifies the global 'n' (making it 6). The 'main' function prints its local 'n' (10) and then the global 'n' (6).

questions.text: Which of these function signatures is different from `void print(int x, double y)`?

choices.label: void print(int a, double b)
choices.text: void print(int a, double b)

choices.label: int print(int x, double y)
choices.text: int print(int x, double y)

choices.label: void print(double x, int y)
choices.text: void print(double x, int y)

choices.label: Both A and B
choices.text: Both A and B

questions.explanation: A and B have the same parameter types in the same order. Changing parameter names (A) or return type (B) does not change the signature. C changes the order of types, making it a different signature.

choices.label: Infinite loop
choices.text: Infinite loop

questions.explanation: Because 'z' is a reference to 'm', every recursive call increments the original 'm'. It starts at 1, becomes 2, then becomes 3, at which point the recursion stops.
